verbatim_text: OK, so what happens when we put all these pieces together? So at the beginning of the talk, we saw a bunch of examples of failures with AI systems. And then I said these could be repaired by enabling AI systems to build coherent and updatable world user and self models. In the talk, I showed examples of two of these by equipping language models with external user models. We can get better specification inference and by training language models to build coherent self models, we could get more faithful explanations. However, many of these broader challenges remain open problems. And going forward, I think world user and self models remains a unifying framework for expanding which failures we can address. Beyond fixing existing errors, though, I am excited to explore new capabilities that these models could enable. So I think one of the end goals of this line of research is to build more trustworthy and collaborative AI systems. And this enables bidirectionally that humans can learn from AI and that AI can also adapt to humans. So, for example, with better self models, we can get build AI systems that can describe their own failure modes, which allow us to better monitor when these systems might misbehave. Second, if we can extract world models from our AI systems, then we can potentially get to a future where we can extract scientific knowledge from systems trained on large scale scientific or simulation corpora. Also, user and self models help us address the specification problem, which we saw earlier. But I think even if we get to a future where systems are theoretically perfect at executing tasks, they still need to infer accurate specifications.
